C1 Particles.question: Define the term relative atomic mass. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The average mass of atoms of an element compared to carbon-12 / compared to 1/12 of a carbon-12 atom

C1 Particles.question: State the relative mass of a proton. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: 1 / approximately 1

C1 Particles.question: What is the charge on an electron? [1 mark]
mark_scheme: -1 / negative

C1 Particles.question: Name the subatomic particle with no charge. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Neutron

C1 Particles.question: Calculate the number of neutrons in an atom of chlorine-35. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Atomic number of chlorine = 17 (1 mark)
35 - 17 = 18 neutrons (1 mark)

C1 Particles.question: Explain why isotopes of the same element have similar chemical properties. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Isotopes have the same number of protons / electrons (1 mark)
Chemical properties are determined by the electron configuration / electrons in the outer shell (1 mark)

C1 Particles.question: An atom of nitrogen-14 contains 7 protons and 7 neutrons. State the number of electrons in a neutral nitrogen atom and explain your answer. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: 7 electrons (1 mark)
In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons / atomic number (1 mark)

C1 Particles.question: Describe how the mass number and atomic number of an atom are related to its protons, neutrons and electrons. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Mass number = protons + neutrons (1 mark)
Atomic number = protons / number of protons only (1 mark)
Number of electrons in a neutral atom = atomic number / protons (1 mark)

C1 Particles.question: Explain why the relative atomic mass of chlorine is 35.5, not a whole number. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Chlorine has two naturally occurring isotopes (1 mark)
Chlorine-35 and chlorine-37 (1 mark)
The relative atomic mass is the weighted average of the mass numbers of all naturally occurring isotopes (1 mark)

C1 Particles.question: An ion X²⁻ has 18 electrons and 17 neutrons. Identify the element X and determine its mass number. Explain your reasoning. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: If X²⁻ has 18 electrons, then neutral X has 16 electrons (1 mark)
Therefore, atomic number = 16, so X is sulfur (1 mark)
Mass number = protons + neutrons = 16 + 17 = 33 (1 mark)
The ion is sulfur-33 / ³³S²⁻ (1 mark)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Define what is meant by an element. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A substance made of only one type of atom / atoms of the same atomic number

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: State what a compound is. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A substance made of two or more elements chemically combined in fixed proportions

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Name an element that is a non-metal at room temperature. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Oxygen / nitrogen / chlorine / sulfur / carbon / phosphorus / iodine (or any other non-metal)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: State one property of ionic compounds. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: High melting point / conduct electricity when molten / conduct electricity when dissolved / brittle / soluble in water

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Explain the difference between a compound and a mixture. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: A compound has elements chemically combined in fixed proportions while a mixture has substances physically combined in variable proportions (1 mark)
Compounds have fixed properties while mixtures can have variable properties / a new substance is formed in compounds but not in mixtures (1 mark)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Describe how you could separate a mixture of salt and water using evaporation. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Heat the salt solution gently / warm (1 mark)
Water evaporates leaving salt crystals / the salt remains (1 mark)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Explain why oxygen is classified as an element and carbon dioxide is classified as a compound. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Oxygen consists of only one type of atom / has only one atomic number (1 mark)
Carbon dioxide consists of two different elements (carbon and oxygen) chemically bonded together (1 mark)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: State the steps used to separate a mixture of sand, salt and water. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Filter to separate sand from the salt solution (1 mark)
Evaporate to separate salt from the water (1 mark)
Alternative: add water to dissolve salt, filter to remove sand, evaporate to recover salt (1 mark for clear method)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: Explain why covalent compounds generally have lower melting points than ionic compounds. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Covalent compounds are molecular with molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces (1 mark)
Ionic compounds have strong ionic bonds between ions (1 mark)
Less energy is therefore required to break the weak forces in covalent compounds than the strong forces in ionic compounds (1 mark)

C2 Elements, compounds and mixtures.question: A sample contains sodium chloride, calcium carbonate and water. Describe a method to separate all three substances. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Filter the mixture to separate calcium carbonate (solid) from the solution (1 mark)
Heat the filtrate / solution to evaporate the water (1 mark)
This leaves sodium chloride crystals (1 mark)
The calcium carbonate can be washed and dried / all three substances are now separated (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: State the definition of a chemical reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A process in which substances are changed into different substances with new properties

C3 Chemical reactions.question: Name one sign that a chemical reaction has occurred. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Temperature change / colour change / gas produced / precipitate formed

C3 Chemical reactions.question: What is meant by thermal decomposition? [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Breaking down a compound into simpler substances using heat

C3 Chemical reactions.question: State what law is represented by the equation: reactants = products. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Law of conservation of mass

C3 Chemical reactions.question: Write the word equation for the combustion of methane. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: methane + oxygen → (1 mark)
carbon dioxide + water (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: Explain why mass is conserved in a chemical reaction. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Atoms are not created or destroyed in a chemical reaction (1 mark)
The atoms are rearranged to form new substances / the same atoms that are present at the start must be present at the end (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: Balance the following equation: Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃ [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Identify the unbalanced atoms (1 mark)
4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃ (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: A student burns 2.0 g of carbon in 10 g of oxygen. The mass of carbon dioxide produced is 7.3 g. Use the law of conservation of mass to explain this result. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Total mass of reactants = 2.0 + 10 = 12.0 g (1 mark)
Not all oxygen reacted (1 mark)
Mass of unreacted oxygen = 12.0 - 7.3 = 4.7 g / more oxygen remained than needed (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: Describe what happens when copper is heated strongly in air and explain the observations in terms of a chemical reaction. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: The copper turns black / a solid black product forms (1 mark)
Copper reacts with oxygen in the air / copper + oxygen → copper oxide (1 mark)
This is a chemical reaction because new substances are formed with different properties (1 mark)

C3 Chemical reactions.question: The thermal decomposition of limestone occurs according to this equation: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂. Explain why this is a decomposition reaction and describe the practical procedures to detect the products. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: One compound (calcium carbonate) breaks down into two simpler substances (1 mark)
Heat is required to break the bonds / thermal decomposition means breaking down with heat (1 mark)
CO₂ is detected by passing gas through limewater which turns cloudy / milky (1 mark)
CaO remains as a white solid residue which when water is added reacts exothermically (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Define an oxidation reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A reaction in which a substance gains oxygen / loses electrons

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: State what type of reaction involves the formation of a precipitate. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Precipitation reaction

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Name the salt produced when hydrochloric acid reacts with sodium hydroxide. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Sodium chloride

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: In a neutralisation reaction, state what is produced in addition to a salt. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Water

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Write the word equation for the reaction between calcium and oxygen. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: calcium + oxygen → (1 mark)
calcium oxide (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Predict the products of the reaction between magnesium carbonate and sulfuric acid. Write the word equation. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: magnesium carbonate + sulfuric acid → (1 mark)
magnesium sulfate + water + carbon dioxide (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: A student adds solutions of sodium carbonate and calcium nitrate to observe a reaction. Describe what they would observe and write the word equation. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: A white precipitate forms (1 mark)
sodium carbonate + calcium nitrate → (1 mark)
calcium carbonate + sodium nitrate (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Explain why copper does not react with dilute hydrochloric acid but zinc does. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Zinc is more reactive than copper / zinc is higher in the reactivity series (1 mark)
Zinc is more easily oxidised / loses electrons more readily (1 mark)
Copper is not reactive enough to displace hydrogen from the acid (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: Write the word equation and symbol equation for the reaction between sodium and water. Identify the type of reaction. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: sodium + water → sodium hydroxide + hydrogen (1 mark)
2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂ (1 mark)
Oxidation reaction / displacement reaction / redox reaction (1 mark)

C4 Predicting and identifying reactions.question: A metal X reacts with dilute sulfuric acid to produce hydrogen gas and a salt. A more reactive metal Y does not react with the salt solution of metal X. Explain what this tells you about the relative reactivity of metals X and Y, and predict what would happen if metal Y was added to a solution of metal X's salt. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Metal X is more reactive than metal Y / X is higher in the reactivity series (1 mark)
This is because Y cannot displace X from its salt solution (1 mark)
If Y is added to X's salt solution, no reaction would occur (1 mark)
Because X is more reactive than Y, so X cannot be displaced from its salt by Y (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Define the term rate of reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The speed at which reactants are converted into products / how much product is formed per unit time

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: State one factor that increases the rate of a reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Increasing temperature / increasing concentration / increasing pressure / using a catalyst / increasing surface area

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: What is meant by a catalyst? [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A substance that speeds up a reaction without being used up / without being chemically changed

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Name one industrial process that uses a catalyst. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Haber process / contact process / cracking of hydrocarbons

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Explain why increasing the temperature of a reaction increases the rate of reaction. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Particles move faster at higher temperatures (1 mark)
This causes more frequent collisions / collisions with greater energy, so more particles have sufficient energy to react (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Explain why crushing a solid reactant into powder increases the rate of reaction. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Crushing increases the surface area of the solid (1 mark)
The increased surface area allows more collisions between reactant particles to occur per unit time (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Describe an experiment to investigate how the concentration of an acid affects the rate of reaction with a metal. Include how you would measure the rate of reaction. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Use the same mass/size of metal with hydrochloric acid of different concentrations (1 mark)
Keep temperature and volume constant (1 mark)
Measure the volume of hydrogen gas produced at regular time intervals / measure the time to produce a fixed volume of gas (1 mark)
Plot a graph of volume against time / compare the gradients of the curves (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: A reaction produces a gas. Describe how you could measure the average rate of reaction by collecting the gas. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Collect the gas in a gas syringe / measuring cylinder / graduated tube (1 mark)
Measure the total volume of gas produced (1 mark)
Record the time taken / calculate rate = volume ÷ time (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: Two identical pieces of zinc are added to two beakers containing hydrochloric acid at different temperatures. Beaker A is at 25°C and Beaker B is at 50°C. Explain why the reaction will be faster in Beaker B and predict what other safety considerations would be important. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: At higher temperature particles have more kinetic energy and move faster (1 mark)
This leads to more frequent and energetic collisions (1 mark)
Safety: heat source control / eye protection / fume hood if toxic gases / management of exothermic reaction (1 mark)

C5 Monitoring and controlling chemical r.question: An enzyme is used as a catalyst in an industrial process. Explain why enzymes are particularly useful catalysts and describe one limitation of using an enzyme compared to a traditional chemical catalyst. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Enzymes are specific and only catalyse reactions they are designed for / highly efficient (1 mark)
They work at lower temperatures than traditional catalysts (1 mark)
Limitation: enzymes are denatured at high temperatures (1 mark)
Or: enzymes are expensive / difficult to produce (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: Name one fossil fuel. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Coal / oil / natural gas

C6 Global challenges.question: State one non-renewable energy resource. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Coal / oil / natural gas / uranium

C6 Global challenges.question: Write the word equation for the combustion of coal. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: coal (carbon) + oxygen → carbon dioxide

C6 Global challenges.question: Give one environmental problem caused by burning fossil fuels. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Produces carbon dioxide which causes global warming / increases greenhouse gases / produces sulfur dioxide which causes acid rain

C6 Global challenges.question: Explain why scientists are concerned about rising carbon dioxide levels in the atmosphere. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas (1 mark)
It traps heat which leads to global warming / climate change (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: Describe how limestone can be used to reduce the effects of acid rain when sulfur dioxide is released into the atmosphere. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Limestone (calcium carbonate) is added to neutralise acidic gases / reacts with sulfur dioxide (1 mark)
The neutralisation reduces the acidity of the rain / prevents damage to ecosystems (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: Explain why sustainable agriculture using renewable resources is preferable to relying on traditional extraction of fossil fuels. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Fossil fuels are non-renewable and will eventually run out (1 mark)
Burning fossil fuels produces greenhouse gases that contribute to climate change (1 mark)
Renewable resources can be replenished / sustainable methods do not deplete resources (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: Describe the steps in the nitrogen cycle and explain why nitrogen compounds are important for agriculture. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Nitrogen from atmosphere is converted to nitrogen compounds by nitrogen-fixing bacteria / lightning (1 mark)
These are used by plants for protein synthesis and growth (1 mark)
Without sufficient nitrogen, crops cannot grow efficiently / nitrogen fertilisers are essential for food production (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: The Haber process converts nitrogen and hydrogen into ammonia under specific conditions. Explain why ammonia is important globally and describe the environmental challenges of producing a large-scale supply. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Ammonia is used to produce nitrogen fertilisers required for global food production (1 mark)
Without ammonia fertilisers, crop yields would be significantly reduced (1 mark)
Producing ammonia requires high temperature, high pressure and uses fossil fuels which produce carbon dioxide (1 mark)
Finding sustainable methods and renewable energy sources for the Haber process is a global challenge (1 mark)

C6 Global challenges.question: Evaluate the use of synthetic fertilisers compared to organic farming methods in terms of environmental impact, food production efficiency and sustainability. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Synthetic fertilisers increase crop yields efficiently / feed a growing population (1 mark)
However, excess fertilisers runoff into waterways causing eutrophication (1 mark)
Organic farming is more sustainable and avoids chemical pollution (1 mark)
But organic farming produces lower yields so requires more land / is less efficient to feed the global population (1 mark)
